_meta.description: Bill 96 (Loi sur la langue officielle et commune du Québec, le français) — French language requirements for businesses in Quebec. Covers commercial signage, customer communications, employment, and compliance timelines.

title_en: Bill 96 — French Language Requirements for Quebec Businesses
title_fr: Loi 96 — Obligations linguistiques des entreprises québécoises
plain_language_summary: Bill 96 (passed 2022, fully in effect 2025-2026) strengthened Quebec's French language requirements for businesses. For most micro-businesses and sole proprietors, the main impact is on: commercial signage (French must be markedly predominant), customer communications (must be offered in French), and any written contracts with clients. The law is more demanding on larger employers.

who_is_affected.all_businesses: All businesses operating in Quebec must offer services in French and use French in commercial signage
who_is_affected.businesses_with_employees: Additional language requirements for workplaces with 25+ employees (francization program)
who_is_affected.micro_businesses: Sole proprietors and very small businesses — mainly affects signage, client communications, and contracts

key_requirements_by_category.applies_to: All businesses with physical presence (storefront, market stall, delivery vehicle with business name)
requirements: French must be markedly predominant on all public-facing signage
'Markedly predominant' generally means French text is twice as large as any other language text
Any slogan, tagline, or motto must appear in French
Business name: if it contains non-French words, an explanatory French descriptor should accompany it
French must appear first in any bilingual text
key_requirements_by_category.plain_language: Your signs must be primarily in French. You can add English (or Arabic, etc.) but the French must be bigger and first. If your business name is in English (e.g., 'Marcus Tech Solutions'), you may need a French descriptor added.
key_requirements_by_category.enforcement: OQLF (Office québécois de la langue française) can receive complaints and inspect compliance
key_requirements_by_category.penalty: Warnings first; fines up to $30,000 for repeat violations

requirements: Clients and customers have the right to be served in French
Websites serving Quebec customers must be available in French
Marketing materials, catalogues, and brochures must be available in French
Order forms and contracts with consumers must be in French
key_requirements_by_category.plain_language: You must be able to communicate with customers in French. You can also offer English service — but French must be available. Your website must have a French version if you serve Quebec clients.
key_requirements_by_category.note: This doesn't mean employees must speak French to internal team members — it means external customer-facing communications must be in French

requirements: Consumer contracts must be provided in French by default
Clients can choose to have their contract in another language — but you must first offer French
Standard form contracts (terms and conditions) must be in French
key_requirements_by_category.plain_language: If you have a service agreement or contract with clients, you need a French version. The client can sign an English version if they prefer — but you must offer French first.
key_requirements_by_category.b2b_note: Business-to-business contracts have more flexibility — both parties can agree to operate in English

requirements: Product names and descriptions must be in French
Instructions and safety warnings must be in French (and equally prominent as any other language)
key_requirements_by_category.plain_language: If you sell a product, the label needs French. For home food businesses, this means ingredients, product names, and any instructions must be in French. English can be added alongside.

requirements: Must register with OQLF and implement a francization program
Must conduct workplace activities in French
Cannot require knowledge of a language other than French for employment (with limited exceptions)
key_requirements_by_category.plain_language: Most micro-businesses won't hit this threshold. If you have 25+ employees, this is significant — consult an HR advisor.
key_requirements_by_category.micro_business_note: Not applicable to sole proprietors and very small businesses

micro_business_practical_guide.title: What Bill 96 Means for You Specifically

what_to_do: Ensure your website has a French version (or at minimum French language option)
Use French in any marketing materials and social media
Have a French version of your service contract/agreement
Respond to clients in French if they write in French
All market signage and price tags in French (prominently)
Can also show English/Arabic pricing — French must be larger/first
All exterior and interior signage French-predominant
Staff must be able to serve customers in French
Order confirmation emails in French (or matching client language preference)

compliance_resources.name: OQLF (Office québécois de la langue française)
compliance_resources.plain_language: The government body that enforces Bill 96. Their website has guides and a free consultation service for businesses.

compliance_resources.name: OQLF Business Guide
compliance_resources.note: Specific guide for small businesses on Bill 96 compliance

compliance_resources.name: Éducaloi — Language Rights Plain Language Explainer

common_mistakes: Having an English-only website and thinking it's fine because your personal language is English — Quebec clients have the right to French service
Using English-only signage at market stalls — OQLF does inspect farmers' markets
Not having a French version of your client service contract — required for consumer contracts
Thinking Bill 96 only applies to large companies — it applies to all businesses in Quebec for signage and client communications
Having a business name in English without a French descriptor — OQLF may require you to add one

free_resources: DeepL (deepl.com) — high quality for French-English translation
Google Translate — adequate for simple documents
translations_help.professional_services: For legally important documents (contracts, terms), consider a professional translator — freelance translators typically charge $0.10-$0.20/word

government_body: Office québécois de la langue française (OQLF)
